description: A reader follows written marks and receives what they carry. 'theo' covers a book, sign, letter, or other readable material; it says that the writing is decoded, not that the reader understands or agrees with it. 'shelomu' (understand) makes that further claim.
articulatory_notes: For stressed 'the', rest the tongue tip at the upper teeth while unvoiced air flows around it and opens into middle-height front 'e'. Do not glide into the next vowel; draw the tongue back and round the lips for a separate middle-height 'o'.
sound_symbolism: Both 'theo' and 'thekiro' (write) begin with the same dental breath and front vowel. The shorter form opens directly into rounded 'o', giving the receiving half of the pair its own compact contour.
usage_notes: The text or other readable object normally precedes the verb, though shared context can leave it unspoken. Aspect distinguishes reading begun, in progress, completed, or habitual. The event-noun rule supplies reading as an activity.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Written speech lets a reader pause before answering and return to the same wording. 'theo' names that reception without claiming patience or agreement on the reader's behalf.

semantic_domains.cognition: decoding written marks as language or another readable system
semantic_domains.communication: receiving an expression preserved in readable form
